title: Cars & Coffee at Pérez Art Museum This Saturday: Here's What to Expect
excerpt: PAMM's monthly Cars & Coffee is back this weekend with an expected 150+ vehicles lining the waterfront. From air-cooled Porsches to brand-new Revueltos, here's your guide to one of Miami's best morning car meets.
content:
If you haven't been to Cars & Coffee at the Pérez Art Museum Miami (PAMM), you're missing one of the best-kept secrets in Miami's car scene. Every first Saturday of the month, the museum's waterfront parking area transforms into a curated open-air car show with Biscayne Bay as the backdrop.

What to Expect

Last month's event drew over 150 vehicles, and this Saturday is expected to be even bigger. The usual suspects — a rotating cast of 911s, a handful of Ferraris, and enough G-Wagons to invade a small country — will be joined by some special appearances. Word on the street is that a local collector is bringing his newly delivered Lamborghini Revuelto, one of the first in South Florida.

The Vibe

Unlike some of Miami's flashier meets, PAMM Cars & Coffee has a more curated, relaxed atmosphere. Think less rev-battle and more genuine conversation between enthusiasts. You'll find everyone from 20-year-old JDM fanatics to 70-year-old Ferrari collectors, and that's exactly what makes it special.

Practical Info

Time: 8 AM – 11 AM (show up by 7:30 if you want to display your car)

Location: Pérez Art Museum Miami, 1103 Biscayne Blvd

Parking: The museum garage opens at 7 AM. Street parking along Biscayne is free before 9 AM on weekends.

Tips: Bring sunscreen (there's minimal shade), grab a coffee from the museum café, and don't forget your camera. The lighting off the bay in the morning is photographer's paradise.

After the Meet

Most attendees roll out to Wynwood for brunch after. Kyu, Salty Donut, and Zak the Baker are all within a 10-minute cruise. It's become an unofficial tradition — cars, coffee, then carbs.

See you Saturday. 🏎️

title: Account Spotlight: @rfrsh.miami — South Florida's Cleanest Car Builds
excerpt: From widebody GT3s to slammed NSXes, this Miami-based Instagram account curates the best modified car content in South Florida. Here's why you should be following them.
content:
In a sea of Miami car content accounts, @rfrsh.miami stands out for one simple reason: taste. While other accounts chase clout with burnout videos and rev battles, RFRSH focuses on what actually matters — the builds.

What They Do

Run by a group of local car enthusiasts, @rfrsh.miami documents South Florida's best modified and exotic cars through clean photography and detailed build features. Their feed is a masterclass in automotive photography — dramatic lighting, interesting angles, and a consistent aesthetic that makes scrolling through their grid genuinely enjoyable.

Recent Highlights

The Bayside Blue R34 GT-R: They recently featured a legally imported Nissan Skyline R34 GT-R that showed up at the Wynwood meet. The photos captured every detail of the car's N1 motor and original interior. This is the kind of car content that makes you stop scrolling.

Widebody 992 GT3 on Brixton Forged: A local owner's Guards Red GT3 with a full Manthey Racing kit and custom Brixton Forged wheels. The shoot was done at sunrise on Key Biscayne — stunning backdrop for a stunning car.

1973 Porsche 911 Carrera RS Tribute: A backdated 911 build done by a Hialeah shop, shot in front of the Marine Stadium. Old school meets old school.

Liberty Walk NSX: A first-gen Acura NSX with the full Liberty Walk widebody treatment, sitting on air suspension in the Design District. Controversial? Maybe. Photogenic? Absolutely.

Why Follow

If you care about car culture — not just car flexing — this is the account to follow. They also host occasional meetups and photo shoots, so it's a great way to connect with the local scene.

Give them a follow: @rfrsh.miami on Instagram

title: Just Landed at RMC Miami: A 2024 McLaren 750S Spider in Volcano Yellow
excerpt: One of Miami's top exotic dealers just took delivery of a head-turning McLaren 750S Spider in the rare Volcano Yellow spec. We've got the details on this $380K open-top supercar.
content:
If you've driven past RMC Miami on NW 36th Street recently, you might have noticed something blindingly yellow in their showroom. That would be the brand-new 2024 McLaren 750S Spider that just landed — and it's a spec that'll stop traffic even by Miami standards.

The Spec

Exterior: Volcano Yellow — one of McLaren's most vibrant MSO colors. It's the kind of paint that photographs itself.

Interior: Carbon Black Alcantara with yellow contrast stitching and a carbon fiber steering wheel. The electrochromic roof panel goes from transparent to opaque at the push of a button.

Performance: The 750S packs McLaren's 4.0-liter twin-turbo V8, now producing 740 horsepower and 590 lb-ft of torque. 0-60 in 2.7 seconds. Top speed: 206 mph with the roof down. It's not just fast — it's McLaren's most powerful series-production convertible ever.

Why This Spec Matters

Volcano Yellow is rarely ordered in Miami, where most buyers gravitate toward silvers, whites, and blacks. This makes it a standout spec — both for driving and as a collector piece. The 750S replaced the 720S and brought significant improvements in aero, chassis dynamics, and that glorious engine tune.

About RMC Miami

RMC (formerly known as Riva Motorsports & Marine) has quietly become one of South Florida's most trusted exotic dealers. Their inventory regularly includes McLarens, Ferraris, Lamborghinis, and Bentleys, with a reputation for transparency and well-documented vehicle histories.

Price: Listed at approximately $380,000. Contact RMC Miami for details.

If yellow isn't your thing, they've also got a Silica White 750S Coupe and a McLaren Artura in Flux Green on the floor. Worth a visit.

title: The Cars of Miami Vice: A Complete Guide to Every Iconic Ride
excerpt: From the fake Daytona Spyder to the real Testarossa, Miami Vice didn't just define 1980s television — it transformed Miami's relationship with exotic cars forever. Here's the complete automotive history of the show.
content:
The Car That Started It All: The "Daytona"

When Miami Vice premiered in September 1984, Sonny Crockett wasn't driving a Ferrari at all. His black sports car was actually a 1972 De Tomaso Mangusta-based replica of a Ferrari 365 GTS/4 Daytona Spyder, built by McBurnie Coachcraft in California. It looked the part on screen, but Ferrari was not amused.

Enzo Ferrari himself reportedly hated that a fake Ferrari was the star of America's hottest show. So Ferrari did something unprecedented: they gave the production two brand-new Testarossas. For free. The only condition? Destroy the replica.

The White Testarossa

In the Season 3 premiere ("When Irish Eyes Are Crying," 1986), the fake Daytona was spectacularly blown up, and Crockett rolled into the next scene in a gleaming white 1986 Ferrari Testarossa. Television history was made.

The white Testarossa became arguably the most famous car in TV history. Its side strakes, pop-up headlights, and flat-12 soundtrack became synonymous with 1980s Miami. Ferrari reportedly saw a 40% increase in US sales the year after the Testarossa appeared on the show.

Two Testarossas were used during filming — one hero car for close-ups and one stunt car. After the show ended in 1989, the hero car was donated to a museum. It sold at Barrett-Jackson in 2015 for $1.95 million.

Tubbs' Cadillac

Ricardo Tubbs (Philip Michael Thomas) drove a 1964 Cadillac Coupe DeVille convertible in black. While not as flashy as Crockett's rides, it was perfectly suited to the character — smooth, understated, and effortlessly cool.

The Villain Cars

Miami Vice was equally famous for its villain vehicles. Drug lords rolled in black Lamborghini Countachs, white Rolls-Royce Corniche convertibles, and the occasional Porsche 928. The show established the visual language that Miami still speaks: exotic cars equal power.

The Legacy

Before Miami Vice, South Florida wasn't particularly known for its car culture. After Miami Vice, it became the exotic car capital of America. Dealers like Prestige Imports and Braman saw a boom in exotic sales. The show essentially created the market that places like RMC Miami, Curated, and South Beach Exotic Rentals now serve.

Today, you can still spot white Testarossas cruising Ocean Drive — a living tribute to the show that made Miami and exotic cars inseparable. 🌴

title: Why Pre-2000 Porsche 911s Are Exploding in Value in Miami
excerpt: Air-cooled 911s have gone from $30K driver's cars to six-figure collectibles in under a decade. Miami is ground zero for this market — here's why, and what you should buy before prices climb higher.
content:
If you've been eyeing an air-cooled Porsche 911, the bad news is you're already late. The good news? There might still be time — if you know where to look and what to buy.

The Numbers

Let's talk data. A clean 1989 Porsche 911 Carrera (964 generation) that sold for $35,000 in Miami in 2016 is now worth $85,000-$120,000 depending on spec and condition. A 1995 993 Carrera that was a $45,000 car five years ago now commands $90,000-$150,000. 993 Turbo S? You're looking at $350,000+ for a good one.

The air-cooled 911 market has appreciated roughly 15-20% annually in South Florida over the past five years — outperforming the S&P 500, real estate, and most alternative investments.

Why Miami?

Several factors make Miami the epicenter of this market:

Climate: No salt, no snow, no rust. Florida cars survive decades without the corrosion that kills northeastern examples. A "Florida car" commands a 10-15% premium nationally.

Demographics: Miami's influx of wealth from tech, crypto, finance, and Latin American money has created a deep pool of buyers who want tangible, enjoyable assets.

Community: Miami has one of the most active air-cooled Porsche communities in the US. Shops like Finsport and Lüfteknic (not far in Atlanta) keep these cars running perfectly.

What to Buy Now

Best value: 1985-1989 Carrera 3.2 (G-body). Still findable under $80K in Miami. The last of the "affordable" air-cooleds.

Best investment: 1995-1998 993 Carrera S or 4S. The last air-cooled 911, wide body, increasingly rare. $120-180K now, likely $200K+ within 3 years.

Dream buy: 1973 Carrera RS 2.7. If you can find one under $1M, buy it immediately.

Where to Shop

In South Florida, check Suncoast Parts (they know every air-cooled 911 for sale within 100 miles), Bring a Trailer for transparent auction pricing, and the PCA Gold Coast Region classifieds. Avoid sight-unseen purchases — always get a PPI from a Porsche specialist.

title: Inside Curated: Miami's Most Exclusive Exotic Car Gallery
excerpt: Part dealer, part art gallery, part private club — Curated in Miami is redefining how the ultra-wealthy buy exotic cars. We got a look inside their invite-only showroom.
content:
You won't find Curated on any car dealer map. There's no flashing signage, no balloon arch, no "SALE" banners. From the outside, their Miami location looks more like a contemporary art gallery. That's entirely by design.

The Concept

Founded by a group of collectors who were tired of the traditional dealer experience, Curated operates more like a private members' club than a car dealership. Their inventory reads like a car enthusiast's fantasy: Pagani Huayras, Ferrari Monzas, McLaren Sennas, and the occasional hypercar that hasn't even been publicly announced yet.

The showroom itself is museum-quality. Cars are displayed on raised platforms with gallery-style lighting. Each vehicle comes with a digital provenance package — full ownership history, service records, and a condition report that would satisfy Christie's auction house.

What Sets Them Apart

Curation over volume: While traditional exotic dealers might stock 50-100 cars, Curated typically has 15-20 at any time. Every car is hand-selected, inspected, and detailed to concours standards before it hits the floor.

Privacy: Transactions are discreet. Many of their clients are high-profile individuals who don't want their purchases splashed across social media. Curated offers private viewing appointments and white-glove delivery anywhere in the world.

Community: They host exclusive events for clients — private track days at Homestead, invite-only dinners, and preview events for incoming inventory. It's as much a social club as a business.

Current Highlights

At the time of writing, their collection reportedly includes a Porsche 918 Spyder with under 500 miles, a Ferrari LaFerrari in Bianco Avus, and one of the few Bugatti Chiron Pur Sports delivered to the US. Total showroom value? Conservatively north of $15 million.

Visit them at curatedcollection.com

title: Supercar Saturdays Bal Harbour: The Ultimate Guide for First-Timers
excerpt: Thinking about checking out Supercar Saturdays at Bal Harbour? Here's everything you need to know — when to arrive, where to park, what to expect, and the unwritten rules of Miami's biggest weekly car meet.
content:
Supercar Saturdays at Bal Harbour Shops is the gold standard of Miami's weekly car meet scene. Every Saturday morning, hundreds of car enthusiasts descend on the luxury shopping center's parking area to show off, admire, and talk cars. Here's your first-timer's guide.

The Basics

When: Every Saturday, roughly 8 AM to 11 AM. The real action starts around 8:30.

Where: Bal Harbour Shops, 9700 Collins Ave, Bal Harbour FL 33154. The car meet happens in the main parking lot.

Cost: Free to attend, free to display your car.

When to Arrive

If you want to display your car: Arrive by 7:30 AM. The best spots go fast, and the organizers (informally) direct traffic to group similar cars together. Exotics tend to cluster near the main entrance.

If you're just spectating: 9 AM is the sweet spot. All the cars are in place, the crowd is buzzing, and you've got a solid hour before things start wrapping up.

What to Expect

On any given Saturday, you'll see 100-200+ vehicles. The typical mix includes:

Exotics: Ferraris, Lamborghinis, McLarens, and the occasional Pagani or Koenigsegg. Miami has one of the highest concentrations of exotic cars per capita in the US.

Classics: Vintage Porsches, muscle cars, and restored American classics. The classic contingent is smaller but always impressive.

Modified: Built Supras, GT-Rs, widebody BMWs, and the South Florida JDM scene in full force.

Luxury: Rolls-Royces, Bentleys, and enough Mercedes-AMGs to fill a dealership.

Unwritten Rules

🚫 Don't touch the cars. Seriously. Don't lean on them, don't open doors, don't sit in them unless invited.

📸 Ask before photographing people (cars are fair game).

🔇 No revving battles. The organizers and Bal Harbour security will shut it down fast.

🤝 Talk to people. The whole point is community. Most owners love talking about their cars.

See you next Saturday. 🏎️

title: The 5 Best Exotic Cars Under $150K You Can Buy in Miami Right Now
excerpt: You don't need half a million dollars to own an exotic in Miami. These five cars deliver supercar thrills at (relatively) attainable prices — and they're all available in South Florida right now.
content:
The dream of exotic car ownership is more attainable than you think — especially in Miami, where the sheer volume of luxury vehicles means depreciation works in the buyer's favor. Here are five genuinely exotic cars you can buy in South Florida for under $150K.

1. Porsche 911 Turbo (997.2) — $85,000-$130,000

The 997.2 Turbo might be the greatest all-around sports car ever built. Twin-turbo flat-six making 500 hp, PDK or manual transmission, all-wheel drive that handles Miami's summer downpours, and Porsche reliability. They've bottomed out in depreciation and are starting to climb. Buy now.

Look for: Sport Chrono, PCCB brakes, manual transmission (premium of $15-20K over PDK).

2. Audi R8 V10 (Gen 1) — $75,000-$120,000

The original R8 with the screaming 5.2L V10 is a genuine supercar that you can daily drive without drama. The naturally aspirated V10 revs to 8,700 RPM and sounds like a junior Lamborghini (because it basically is — same engine family as the Gallardo/Huracán).

Look for: V10 Plus models, manual gated shifter (increasingly rare and valuable), carbon ceramic brakes.

3. Ferrari California T — $100,000-$140,000

The California T is Ferrari's most approachable modern model — a twin-turbo V8 grand tourer with a retractable hardtop. Is it the most hardcore Ferrari? No. Is it a gorgeous, comfortable, genuinely fun Ferrari you can drive every day in Miami? Absolutely.

Look for: Handling Speciale package, Daytona-style seats, bright colors (they look best in Rosso Corsa or Blu Pozzi).

4. Lamborghini Gallardo LP560-4 — $90,000-$135,000

The Gallardo is still one of the most visually dramatic cars on the road. The LP560-4 is the sweet spot of the range — 560 hp V10, e-gear or manual, and that unmistakable Lamborghini presence. In Miami, a Gallardo gets nearly as much attention as a Huracán at half the price.

Look for: LP560-4 Spyder for the full Miami experience. Manual cars command a premium but are worth it.

5. Aston Martin DB11 V8 — $110,000-$145,000

The DB11 is the car James Bond would drive if he lived in Brickell. Mercedes-AMG twin-turbo V8, stunning British design, and a grand touring experience that nothing else in this price range can match. Early V8 models have depreciated into our range and they're absolute bargains.

Look for: V8 over V12 (more reliable, better balanced), Launch Edition, any color that isn't silver.

title: Prestige Imports Lamborghini Miami Takes Delivery of the Revuelto — Here's What It Costs
excerpt: South Florida's authorized Lamborghini dealer has received its first allocation of the Revuelto, Lamborghini's 1,001-hp hybrid V12 supercar. The price? Starting at $608,358 — before options.
content:
Prestige Imports on Collins Avenue in North Miami Beach has been the go-to Lamborghini dealer in South Florida for decades. This week, they received their first batch of the most important Lamborghini in a generation: the Revuelto.

The Car

The Revuelto is Lamborghini's first series-production hybrid — and it's a monster. A naturally aspirated 6.5-liter V12 paired with three electric motors produces a combined 1,001 horsepower. Zero to 60 in 2.5 seconds. Top speed: 217 mph. It's the most powerful Lamborghini ever built.

But the headline number doesn't tell the whole story. The Revuelto can drive up to 6 miles on pure electric power — enough to silently cruise through South Beach at 3 AM without waking the neighbors. It's a hypercar that can also be a gentleman.

The Price

Base MSRP starts at $608,358 before destination. But this is Lamborghini — nobody orders a base Revuelto. Add the Ad Personam interior customization ($15-30K), carbon fiber exterior package ($12K), Stile Edition paint ($8K), and the typical Miami buyer's options list, and you're looking at $680,000-$750,000 as delivered.

The Wait

Here's the reality: if you haven't already placed an order, you're looking at a 2+ year wait. Prestige Imports reportedly has a waitlist that extends into 2028. Allocation priority goes to existing Lamborghini owners and VIP clients — as it always does with flagship models.

For those who can't wait, expect the secondary market to command a $100-200K premium over sticker for the first year of deliveries. Several Miami-area Revueltos have already been pre-sold on the secondary market for north of $800K.

See It In Person

Prestige Imports typically displays new flagship arrivals in their main showroom for the first week. If you want to see the Revuelto up close, stop by their showroom at 14800 Biscayne Blvd, North Miami Beach. No appointment needed — just walk in.

More info: prestigeimports.net

title: Spotted in Miami: The Best Exotic Car Sightings This Week
excerpt: From a Bugatti Chiron on Brickell to a convoy of Rolls-Royces in South Beach, it was another wild week for car spotting in Miami. Here are the highlights from @exoticspottermiami.
content:
Every week, Miami delivers automotive eye candy that most cities only see at car shows. Thanks to the eagle-eyed community of local car spotters — especially @exoticspottermiami — we're rounding up the best sightings from the past seven days.

🏆 Sighting of the Week: Bugatti Chiron Sport in Brickell

Spotted Tuesday afternoon on Brickell Avenue near Cipriani, a Bugatti Chiron Sport in Atlantic Blue over French Racing Blue. This is one of approximately 12 Chirons registered in Miami-Dade County. The owner was heading to lunch — casually driving a $3.3 million car to get pasta. Only in Miami.

Rolls-Royce Convoy on Ocean Drive

Saturday night on Ocean Drive brought a convoy of five — yes, five — Rolls-Royces rolling in formation: two Cullinans (one matte black, one white), a Dawn convertible in Salamanca Blue, a Ghost in Arctic White, and bringing up the rear, a Phantom Extended in Bohemian Red. Total fleet value: approximately $2.5 million.

Ferrari SF90 XX Stradale at Bal Harbour

One of Ferrari's most extreme road cars was spotted at the Bal Harbour Shops valet on Thursday. The SF90 XX Stradale, with its 1,016 horsepower and fixed rear wing, is limited to 799 units worldwide. This one wore Rosso Corsa with racing stripes — the classic spec. Estimated value: $850,000+.

Pagani Huayra Roadster on I-95

Perhaps the most surprising sighting: a Pagani Huayra Roadster BC was photographed merging onto I-95 South near the Golden Glades interchange on Wednesday morning. Spec: exposed carbon fiber with blue accents. Only 40 Roadster BCs were made, at approximately $3.5 million each. Where was it heading? Unknown. But wherever it was going, it was getting there in style.

Honorable Mentions

• Matte green Lamborghini Huracán Sterrato on the MacArthur Causeway
• Toyota 2000GT (!!!) at Supercar Saturday — worth over $1 million
• A pristine DeLorean DMC-12 in Wynwood, complete with a flux capacitor prop

Got a sighting? Follow and tag @exoticspottermiami on Instagram.

title: Pagani Utopia Spotted at Bal Harbour — Is This Miami's First?
excerpt: A jaw-dropping Pagani Utopia in exposed carbon with a champagne gold tint was photographed outside Bal Harbour Shops this week. At $3.4 million, it might be the rarest hypercar currently roaming South Florida streets.
content:
Miami's exotic car scene just leveled up. A Pagani Utopia — Horacio Pagani's latest $3.4 million masterpiece — was spotted parked casually outside Bal Harbour Shops earlier this week, and the internet lost its collective mind.

The Spec

This particular Utopia is finished in exposed carbon fiber with a champagne gold tint, riding on custom forged center-lock wheels. The interior? Cognac leather with contrast stitching visible through that iconic glass engine cover. Only around 99 units are being built worldwide, making this one of the rarest production cars on the planet.

View this post on Instagram

The Engine

Under that sculpted rear clamshell sits a Mercedes-AMG sourced 6.0-liter twin-turbo V12 producing 852 horsepower mated to a 7-speed manual or automated manual transmission. Yes, you read that right — in 2026, Pagani still believes in the manual gearbox. And honestly? In a city where every other car is a dual-clutch paddle-shift special, that's incredibly refreshing.

Miami's Hypercar Arms Race

This Utopia joins an increasingly absurd roster of hypercars calling South Florida home. Between the Bugatti Chirons prowling South Beach, the growing fleet of Koenigseggs at private collections in Coral Gables, and now a Pagani Utopia doing grocery runs at Bal Harbour — Miami might genuinely be the hypercar capital of the Western Hemisphere.

Pagani Utopia just casually parked at Bal Harbour like it's a Honda Civic. Only in Miami. 🌴🇮🇹

— Miami Car Spotters (@MiamiCarSpots) March 24, 2026

Where to Spot It

Based on the license plate frame, this Utopia appears to have been delivered through The Collection in Coral Gables — one of the few authorized Pagani dealers in the US. If you want to catch a glimpse, your best bets are Bal Harbour, Brickell, and Supercar Saturdays. Just maybe don't try to race it at the light. 😅

title: The Rise of Miami's JDM Underground: GT-Rs, Supras, and NSXs Are Taking Over
excerpt: Forget Lamborghinis for a second. Miami's Japanese Domestic Market scene is exploding — with R34 Skylines, MK4 Supras, and first-gen NSXs popping up at meets from Homestead to Wynwood.
content:
If your Instagram feed is nothing but Lamborghinis and Ferraris, you're following the wrong Miami car accounts. Quietly — well, not that quietly, given the exhaust notes — Miami's JDM scene has absolutely erupted over the past two years.

The 25-Year Rule Changed Everything

Thanks to the US 25-year import rule, the most legendary Japanese cars from the late '90s and early 2000s are finally street-legal. R34 Nissan Skyline GT-Rs, Mazda RX-7 FD Spirit R editions, Mitsubishi Evo VI Tommi Mäkinens — they're all here now. And Miami, with its no-rust climate and year-round driving weather, has become a magnet for collectors.

View this post on Instagram

The Meets

Every other Friday night, a JDM-focused meet takes over a parking lot in Homestead with 50+ cars. Think rows of Supras in every color, S2000s with hardtops, and the occasional Kei truck that someone imported for laughs. Wynwood's late-night car culture has also embraced JDM — you'll find turbo'd 240SXs parked next to street art murals at 1 AM.

Prices Are Wild

Here's what the market looks like in South Florida right now:

  • R34 GT-R V-Spec II — $350K–$500K (yes, really)
  • MK4 Supra Turbo 6-speed — $120K–$180K
  • NSX-R (NA2) — $250K–$400K
  • FD RX-7 Spirit R — $90K–$150K
  • Evo VI TME — $80K–$120K

These aren't just cars anymore — they're investments. And Miami's wealthy collector base has noticed.

Just saw an R34 GT-R in Bayside Blue rolling through Brickell at midnight. The sound. The wing. The vibes. Peak Miami moment. 🏎️💙

— JDM Miami (@JDMSouthFL) March 23, 2026

Where the Scene Lives

Follow these accounts to stay plugged in:

  • 📸 @jdm_miami — The main feed for local JDM content
  • 📸 @jdmsfl — South Florida builds and meets
  • 📸 @thejdmculture — Culture and lifestyle

Whether you're a lifelong Gran Turismo nerd or just discovering what all the hype is about, Miami's JDM underground is one of the most exciting car scenes in the country right now. Catch it before R34 prices hit seven figures. 🔰

title: The 5 Best Drop-Tops for Miami Living in 2026
excerpt: Year-round sunshine, ocean drives, and palm-lined boulevards — Miami was made for convertibles. Here are the 5 best open-top cars to buy right now, from attainable fun to full-blown exotic.
content:
Miami has exactly two seasons: top down and top down with the AC on. If you live here and don't own a convertible, you're doing it wrong. Here are our picks for the best drop-tops to cruise Ocean Drive, A1A, and everything in between.

1. Porsche 911 Carrera GTS Cabriolet ($180K–$200K)

The 911 Cabriolet is basically the official car of South Beach. The new GTS hybrid makes 532 hp and sounds incredible with the top down. It's fast enough to be exciting, refined enough for daily driving, and — let's be honest — it looks absolutely right cruising Collins Avenue at sunset.

View this post on Instagram

2. Ferrari Roma Spider ($280K–$310K)

The Roma Spider is what happens when Ferrari decides to make a grand tourer that's more about vibes than lap times. The retractable soft top opens in 13.5 seconds, the twin-turbo V8 makes 612 hp, and it might be the most beautiful car Ferrari currently sells. In Miami's Art Deco backdrop? Chef's kiss. 🤌

3. BMW M4 Convertible ($90K–$105K)

If you want serious performance without the supercar price tag, the M4 Convertible is the sweet spot. The twin-turbo straight-six makes 503 hp in Competition trim, the retractable hardtop keeps things quiet on I-95, and it's practical enough to daily drive. Plus, BMW's finally got the design right again — the new front end actually works in person.

4. Chevrolet Corvette E-Ray Convertible ($110K–$125K)

The C8 Corvette was already a steal. The E-Ray — with AWD, a hybrid boost, and a 0-60 of 2.5 seconds — is borderline absurd for the money. Top down, engine behind you, and all-wheel drive for those Miami summer downpours? It's the best value on this list by a mile.

C8 E-Ray Convertible in Riptide Blue rolling through Key Biscayne. That color was MADE for Miami. Best sub-$130K drop-top on the market, fight me. 🐬

— Corvette Miami (@CorvetteMIA) March 24, 2026

5. Mazda MX-5 Miata ($30K–$38K)

Yeah, we said it. Among all the six-figure exotics, the Miata is still king of pure driving fun. At under $40K, it's the most smiles-per-dollar you can buy. Light, tossable, manual transmission, roof folds in one hand — it's everything a Miami convertible should be. Take it through the Rickenbacker Causeway with the top down and tell us it doesn't slap.

The Verdict

Honestly, you can't go wrong with any of these. The Porsche and Ferrari are for the baller crowd, the M4 and E-Ray hit the performance sweet spot, and the Miata proves you don't need to spend a fortune to enjoy Miami's best feature — the weather. Whatever you choose, just please: put the top down. You live in Miami. Act like it. ☀️🌴

title: The Best Performance Parts & Mods for Miami Street Cars in 2026
excerpt: From bolt-on exhaust upgrades to ceramic coatings that survive Miami's brutal sun, here are the best performance parts and mods for your street car — with direct links to grab them today.
content:
Miami's car culture isn't just about buying exotic — it's about building exotic. Whether you're pushing a turbo Supra down US-1 or keeping your daily M4 looking showroom-fresh in 95°F heat, the right parts and mods make all the difference. Here's our curated list of the best upgrades for Miami street cars in 2026.

🌬️ Cold Air Intakes — Breathe Better, Drive Harder

Miami's hot, humid air is the enemy of performance. A cold air intake is one of the simplest bolt-on mods that actually makes a measurable difference — cooler, denser air means more power. Plus, the intake sound under a tunnel on I-95 is *chef's kiss*.

Our pick: The K&N 57 Series FIPK Cold Air Intake is the gold standard. Fitments available for everything from Mustangs to M3s. The washable, reusable filter means you're not buying replacements every 6 months in Miami's dusty construction zones.

For a more budget-friendly universal option, the K&N Universal Clamp-On Air Filter works great on custom turbo setups — popular with the Wynwood JDM crowd.

🛡️ Ceramic Coating — Miami Sun Protection for Your Paint

If you're spending $100K+ on a car and parking it outside in Miami, you need ceramic coating. Period. The UV exposure, salt air, and afternoon rain will destroy unprotected paint in a season. A good ceramic coat provides 2-5 years of protection with insane hydrophobic properties.

DIY pick: CarPro CQuartz UK 3.0 Ceramic Coating Kit — the enthusiast-grade favorite. Professional results without the $1,500+ shop bill. One kit covers a full-size sedan.

Maintenance spray: Between full coatings, the Meguiar's Hybrid Ceramic Wax is a quick spray-on option that adds a ceramic layer during regular washes. Miami detailers swear by it.

View this post on Instagram

📹 Dash Cams — Protect Yourself on I-95

Let's be real: Miami drivers are insane. Between the no-signal lane changes on 836, the scooters weaving through Brickell, and the tourists stopping dead on Ocean Drive — a dash cam isn't optional. It's survival gear. And if you're driving something exotic, you want proof when someone door-dings your Huracán at Publix.

Best overall: The VIOFO A129 Pro Duo 4K Dash Cam — dual camera (front + rear), 4K resolution, GPS tracking, and parking mode that records when your car detects motion while parked. Essential for Miami street parking.

Budget pick: The Vantrue N1 Pro 2K Dash Cam — compact, great night vision, and voice-controlled so you can trigger a save without taking your hands off the wheel.

✨ Wheel Cleaning & Detailing — Keep Those Forged Rims Clean

Brake dust on Miami's stop-and-go highways will eat your wheels alive. If you're running aftermarket Vossen, HRE, or Brixton wheels, proper cleaning isn't vanity — it's maintenance.

Wheel cleaner: Sonax Wheel Cleaner Full Effect — pH-neutral, safe on all finishes, and turns purple as it dissolves brake dust. Deeply satisfying to watch.

Full detailing kit: The Chemical Guys Complete Car Wash & Detailing Kit (16-piece) has everything — wash mitt, clay bar, compound, sealant, tire gel. Perfect starter kit if you're doing weekend details in the driveway.

Tire shine: Chemical Guys VRP Vinyl, Rubber, Plastic Shine Dressing — gives tires that wet-black Miami show look without the greasy sling.

🔧 Performance Exhaust — Sound Matters

In a city where your car is heard before it's seen, exhaust upgrades are practically mandatory. The right system adds power, drops weight, and gives your car a voice that matches its looks.

For specific exhaust recommendations, you'll want to go brand-specific — Akrapovič for European exotics, Borla for American muscle, and Tomei for JDM builds. Most Miami shops like SOS Automotive in Hialeah and Wheels Boutique in Wynwood can source and install. But for universal exhaust tips and accessories, the NETAMI Stainless Steel Exhaust Tip (Black, 2.5" inlet) is a cheap cosmetic upgrade that looks surprisingly legit.

Nothing hits like a cold start in a parking garage at 7 AM. Akrapovič exhaust on the 992 GT3 echoing off concrete walls. Pure therapy. 🎵🏎️

— 305 Car Culture (@305CarCulture) March 25, 2026

🎨 Wraps & Window Tint — Miami Essentials

A quality wrap lets you change your car's personality without touching the factory paint — crucial for resale on exotics. And window tint isn't just about looks in Miami; it's about not melting into your leather seats in August.

Wraps and tint are best done by pros (SunTek and XPEL certified shops are your go-to in Miami), but for DIY paint protection on high-impact areas like door edges, rocker panels, and hood leads, XPEL Ultimate Plus Paint Protection Film (PPF) — 6" x 25' Roll is a solid investment. Apply it to the front bumper and hood to protect against rock chips on the Turnpike.

The Bottom Line

You don't need to spend five figures to meaningfully upgrade your ride. A cold air intake, ceramic coating, and a good dash cam will run you under $500 total and immediately improve your daily driving experience in Miami. Start there, then go deeper as the mod bug inevitably bites. And it will bite. 🔧🏁

Disclosure: Some links above are affiliate links. We earn a small commission at no extra cost to you — it helps keep GridLocal running. We only recommend products we'd actually use on our own cars.

title: Owning an Exotic in Miami: The Real Annual Cost Breakdown
excerpt: Insurance, maintenance, tires, fuel, storage, detailing — what does it actually cost to own a Lamborghini, Ferrari, or Porsche in Miami for a year? We break down every dollar so there are no surprises.
content:
Everyone talks about the purchase price. Nobody talks about what happens after. Owning an exotic car in Miami isn't just about the monthly payment — it's about the ecosystem of costs that come with keeping a supercar alive, legal, and looking good in the 305. Here's the honest, unfiltered breakdown.

💰 The Car We're Using as a Baseline

To keep things concrete, we'll use a 2022 Lamborghini Huracán EVO (LP 610-4) as our reference — a car you can find in Miami for around $230,000-$260,000. It's one of the most common exotics on the road here, so the cost data is well-established.

🛡️ Insurance: $4,000–$8,000/year

This is the one that shocks people most. Miami has some of the highest auto insurance rates in the country — even for normal cars. For a Lamborghini Huracán, expect:

  • Clean record, 35+ years old, garaged: $4,000–$5,500/year
  • Under 30, clean record: $6,500–$8,000/year
  • Under 25 or any incidents: $10,000+ (if you can get coverage at all)

Agreed-value policies from specialists like Hagerty or Chubb are usually better than standard carriers for exotics — they'll pay the car's actual market value in a total loss, not a depreciated estimate.

🔧 Maintenance: $3,000–$7,000/year

Lamborghini's scheduled maintenance isn't as apocalyptic as legend suggests, but it's not cheap either:

  • Annual service (oil change, filters, inspection): $800–$1,200
  • Major service (every 2 years / 10K miles): $2,500–$4,000
  • Brake pads (when needed): $2,000–$3,500 for all four corners
  • Carbon ceramic brake rotors (if equipped): $12,000–$18,000 per set (lasts 50K+ miles)

Annual average for a driven-but-not-tracked Huracán: roughly $4,500/year.

Pro tip: Keep a quality FOXWELL NT301 OBD2 Scanner in your glovebox. It reads and clears engine codes, monitors real-time data, and can save you a $200 dealer diagnostic fee for something as simple as a loose gas cap.

View this post on Instagram

🏎️ Tires: $2,000–$4,000/year

Exotic car tires are wide, sticky, and expensive. The Huracán runs staggered Pirelli P Zero tires — roughly $350–$500 per tire. A full set: $1,400–$2,000. On Miami's rough roads and with spirited driving, expect to replace them every 10,000–15,000 miles — which for most owners means annually.

Florida's summer heat also accelerates tire wear. Keep a AstroAI Digital Tire Pressure Gauge handy — properly inflated tires last significantly longer and perform better. Under-inflation is the #1 tire killer in hot climates.

⛽ Fuel: $3,000–$5,000/year

The Huracán's 5.2L V10 requires 93 octane premium, which runs about $4.50–$5.00/gallon in Miami. At roughly 13-15 MPG combined (and worse if you're enjoying yourself), budget:

  • 8,000 miles/year: ~$2,800–$3,200
  • 12,000 miles/year: ~$4,000–$5,000

Nobody buys a Lamborghini for fuel economy. But it's worth budgeting for.

🏠 Storage: $0–$6,000/year

If you've got a home garage, this costs nothing. But many Miami condo owners don't have that luxury. Options:

  • Condo valet parking: Usually included, but your exotic gets door-ding roulette every day
  • Dedicated indoor storage (climate-controlled): $400–$500/month ($4,800–$6,000/year)
  • Shared enthusiast garages (like Miami Supercar Rooms): $250–$400/month

If you're parking outside — which we don't recommend for anything over $100K — at minimum invest in a quality Covercraft WeatherShield HP Custom Car Cover. It's UV-resistant, breathable, and custom-fitted for your specific model. Miami sun will fade paint and destroy interiors without protection.

✨ Detailing: $1,200–$3,600/year

In Miami, your car is always on display. Salt air, pollen, construction dust, and bird droppings (thanks, iguanas) mean regular detailing isn't vanity — it's preservation.

  • Monthly professional detail: $150–$300/visit
  • Annual paint correction + ceramic coating: $800–$1,500

For between-detail maintenance, keep these in your garage:

  • Meguiar's Hybrid Ceramic Wax — spray-on ceramic protection during regular washes ($15)
  • Sonax Wheel Cleaner Full Effect — keeps brake dust from eating your rims ($18)
  • Chemical Guys Microfiber Wash Mitt — scratch-free washing is non-negotiable on exotic paint ($10)

People ask why I detail my car every two weeks. Because it's a $250K car sitting in Miami sun, salt, and humidity 365 days a year. Maintenance isn't optional. It's respect. 🧽✨

— Miami Exotic Owners (@MIAExoticOwners) March 24, 2026

📋 Registration, Taxes & Misc: $2,000–$4,000/year

  • Florida registration (based on weight): $400–$600/year
  • Personal property tax: Florida doesn't have one — that's a huge perk
  • Parking tickets (let's be real): Budget $200–$500/year if you're driving in Miami Beach and Brickell regularly
  • SunPass / toll roads: $600–$1,200/year if you use the Turnpike, 836, or express lanes

📊 The Grand Total

CategoryLow EstimateHigh Estimate
Insurance$4,000$8,000
Maintenance$3,000$7,000
Tires$2,000$4,000
Fuel$3,000$5,000
Storage$0$6,000
Detailing$1,200$3,600
Registration & Misc$2,000$4,000
TOTAL ANNUAL COST$15,200$37,600

Is It Worth It?

That's $1,267–$3,133 per month on top of your car payment or purchase price. Is it a lot? Yes. Is it more than most people expect? Absolutely. Is it still worth it if you love cars and live in one of the best driving cities in America? 100%.

The key is going in with eyes open. Budget for the real costs, stay on top of maintenance, protect the paint, and you'll enjoy every second of exotic ownership in Miami. The lifestyle here — ocean drives, car meets, Saturday morning cruises to Bal Harbour — makes it all worth it.

Just don't cheap out on insurance. Trust us on that one. 🙏

Disclosure: Some product links above are affiliate links. We earn a small commission at no extra cost to you, which helps support GridLocal. We only recommend products we'd use on our own cars.
